EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Part of western Kentucky experienced its heaviest snowfall for so early in the season. The heaviest snowfall amounts were in counties along the Ohio River, which received anywhere from three to five inches. To the southeast of the Ohio River counties, anywhere from one to three inches fell along a corridor from Fulton and Mayfield to Princeton and Madisonville. Less than an inch was reported along the Tennessee border from Murray to Hopkinsville. The National Weather Service office at Paducah measured 2.8 inches, which was the heaviest snowfall for so early in the season. A weak low pressure system developed over the northern Gulf of Mexico, then moved northeast into Alabama. This system threw moisture northward into unseasonably cold air over the Lower Ohio Valley, resulting in widespread snow. Specific snowfall amounts included 5 inches at Morganfield (Union County), 5 inches about five miles south of Owensboro (Daviess County), 4 inches at Kevil (Ballard County), 3 inches at Paducah (McCracken County), 3 inches at Marion (Crittenden County), 3 inches at Hanson (Hopkins County), 2.5 inches at Eddyville (Lyon County), 2 inches at Dublin (Graves County), and one inch at Hickman (Fulton County). The snow fell at temperatures right near freezing on relatively warm pavement. The highest accumulations were on grassy areas. Roads were slush-covered and slippery during the period of heaviest snow.

Widespread severe thunderstorms downed numerous trees across a large swath of eastern Kentucky during the afternoon and evening hours of September 2nd. Thunderstorms first began forming along an old outflow boundary just before 4 pm on this date. With the amount of moisture and instability in place, it wasn't long before a number of these storms began producing damaging wind gusts. The last of the storms weakened below severe limits just before 7 pm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A storm spotter reported roof and gutter damage to the VFW hall on Main Street in Whitley City.

More flooding occurred across eastern Kentucky during the afternoon and evening hours of September 6th. Thunderstorms formed across central Kentucky ahead of a cold front during the afternoon hours on September 6th. As these storms moved eastward, they ingested moisture rich air that was already in place across eastern Kentucky. This, along with the relatively slow movement of the storms, allowed the storms to produce very heavy rainfall. This rainfall lead to flash flooding across a number of counties across eastern Kentucky on this day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six inches or more of water was flowing across Hwy 15 at the red light in front of the Shell gas station. The flood water was also flowing onto Hwy 191 going through Main and Johnson Streets.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic cold front sweep through northeast Ohio just after midnight on Monday the 6th.   Rain showers and temperatures in the upper 40s and lower 50s quickly became snow showers with temperatures falling through the 20s by the predawn hours.  Snow accumulations were less than 2 inches.  Temperatures continued to fall during the day on the 6th, with blustery winds.  Readings reached down to either side of zero by sunset.  ||Temperatures at dawn on the 7th were mostly around 5 below zero.  Wind chill readings bottomed out in the minus 20 to minus 25 degree range overnight and into the morning hours.||Despite sunshine, temperatures were slow to rise during the day on the 7th.  However, winds did subside during the afternoon.||A scattering of frozen pipes, power outages, home furnace difficulties, and vehicular engine problems occurred during the cold wave.  There were over 100 leaks in the Greenup water system, affecting around 2,500 customers.  Repair companies were kept busy.  County public schools were closed.

An arctic cold front barreled across eastern Kentucky late Sunday January 5th ushering in what would be the coldest temperatures and wind chills experienced in 20 years. Gusty winds up to 50 mph along the front created a few isolated power outages. Rain falling in advance of the arctic front quickly changed over to snow by midnight Sunday night. By dawn on Monday, many areas had received from 1 to 3 inches of snow, with up to 5 inches on Black Mountain. ||The big story was the cold.  Temperatures by Tuesday morning January 7th ranged from 2 below zero to 9 below zero in the valleys of eastern Kentucky, down to 17 below zero at the top of Black Mountain. Gusty west to northwest winds combined with these temperatures to produce wind chills in the 15 to 30 below range for most locations, with the highest terrain along the Virginia border dropping even lower. The last time there were widespread below zero temperatures in eastern Kentucky was in the middle 1990s.||Several southeast Kentucky counties experienced water outages from the cold that lasted up to a week.  The hardest hit counties were Perry and Floyd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Interstate 75 was blocked due to an injury accident as a result of a vehicle loosing control on black ice. The injured motorist later died at the hospital.

Widespread severe thunderstorms downed numerous trees across a large swath of eastern Kentucky during the afternoon and evening hours of September 2nd. Thunderstorms first began forming along an old outflow boundary just before 4 pm on this date. With the amount of moisture and instability in place, it wasn't long before a number of these storms began producing damaging wind gusts. The last of the storms weakened below severe limits just before 7 pm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree that was blown down by thunderstorm wind gusts was blocking Fox Hollow Rd.

Part of western Kentucky experienced its heaviest snowfall for so early in the season. The heaviest snowfall amounts were in counties along the Ohio River, which received anywhere from three to five inches. To the southeast of the Ohio River counties, anywhere from one to three inches fell along a corridor from Fulton and Mayfield to Princeton and Madisonville. Less than an inch was reported along the Tennessee border from Murray to Hopkinsville. The National Weather Service office at Paducah measured 2.8 inches, which was the heaviest snowfall for so early in the season. A weak low pressure system developed over the northern Gulf of Mexico, then moved northeast into Alabama. This system threw moisture northward into unseasonably cold air over the Lower Ohio Valley, resulting in widespread snow. Specific snowfall amounts included 5 inches at Morganfield (Union County), 5 inches about five miles south of Owensboro (Daviess County), 4 inches at Kevil (Ballard County), 3 inches at Paducah (McCracken County), 3 inches at Marion (Crittenden County), 3 inches at Hanson (Hopkins County), 2.5 inches at Eddyville (Lyon County), 2 inches at Dublin (Graves County), and one inch at Hickman (Fulton County). The snow fell at temperatures right near freezing on relatively warm pavement. The highest accumulations were on grassy areas. Roads were slush-covered and slippery during the period of heaviest snow.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were blown down and condos were damaged at the Woodson Bend resort.

An arctic cold front barreled across eastern Kentucky late Sunday January 5th ushering in what would be the coldest temperatures and wind chills experienced in 20 years. Gusty winds up to 50 mph along the front created a few isolated power outages. Rain falling in advance of the arctic front quickly changed over to snow by midnight Sunday night. By dawn on Monday, many areas had received from 1 to 3 inches of snow, with up to 5 inches on Black Mountain. ||The big story was the cold.  Temperatures by Tuesday morning January 7th ranged from 2 below zero to 9 below zero in the valleys of eastern Kentucky, down to 17 below zero at the top of Black Mountain. Gusty west to northwest winds combined with these temperatures to produce wind chills in the 15 to 30 below range for most locations, with the highest terrain along the Virginia border dropping even lower. The last time there were widespread below zero temperatures in eastern Kentucky was in the middle 1990s.||Several southeast Kentucky counties experienced water outages from the cold that lasted up to a week.  The hardest hit counties were Perry and Floyd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Kentucky Mesonet station located four miles south of Owingsville reported a wind chill of 25 below zero.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic cold front sweep through northeast Ohio just after midnight on Monday the 6th.   Rain showers and temperatures in the upper 40s and lower 50s quickly became snow showers with temperatures falling through the 20s by the predawn hours.  Snow accumulations were less than 2 inches.  Temperatures continued to fall during the day on the 6th, with blustery winds.  Readings reached down to either side of zero by sunset.  ||Temperatures at dawn on the 7th were mostly around 5 below zero.  Wind chill readings bottomed out in the minus 20 to minus 25 degree range overnight and into the morning hours.||Despite sunshine, temperatures were slow to rise during the day on the 7th.  However, winds did subside during the afternoon.||A scattering of frozen pipes, power outages, home furnace difficulties, and vehicular engine problems occurred during the cold wave.  There were over 100 leaks in the Greenup water system, affecting around 2,500 customers.  Repair companies were kept busy.  County public schools were closed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Arctic air poured into northeast Kentucky on the 27th.  A clear night with diminishing winds resulted in temperatures on either side of the zero mark at dawn on the 28th.||The coldest dawn temperatures occurred on the 29th.  Preliminary data indicated the coldest temperature from the official cooperative observers network was 9 below zero at Olive Hill.| |Some of the other minimum temperature readings around dawn on the 29th from cooperative observers included 7 below zero at Warnock and 6 below zero west of Louisa.  Ashland observed a minimum temperature of 5 below zero. | |The cold wave lasted one more night, with readings of zero to 5 below zero around dawn on the 30th.  For example, Olive Hill observed minus 4. ||The 3 day cold wave finally broke on Thursday the 30th, as temperatures rose into the 35 to 40 degree range under sunshine. ||As the cold air was pouring in, a 12 inch water line broke along Carter Ave in Ashland Kentucky.  It caused about 5,000 customers in the Ashland, Catlettsburg, and Westwood vicinity to loose water.  The interruption of water service continued for several days.  The city of Ashland handed out bottled water.  The leak also drained the city's storage tank.

A strong temperature gradient existed from southeast to northeast across the mid Ohio Valley on the 4th.  An old snow cover over Ohio and extreme northern Kentucky helped to maintain surface temperatures slightly below freezing.||A low pressure storm system moved out of the Tennessee Valley late on the 4th.  The storm lifted northeast and up the Ohio Valley of eastern Ohio by dawn on the 5th.||A wintry mix of rain, sleet, and freezing rain developed across northeast Kentucky by 1900E on the 4th.  Ice from freezing rain accumulated from a quarter to a half inch across Carter and Greenup Counties before changing to plain rain.  In Lawrence County about a tenth of an inch of ice accumulated across the northern portion of the county mainly north of Route 32.  In the Knotty Poplar Fork area along the Carter County line, some trees were down with the ice reaching near a quarter of an inch.  The brunt of the storm was rain for Boyd and southern and eastern portions of Lawrence Counties.||Tree branches fell, including around Olive Hill of western Carter County and along the Tygarts Creek Valley in western Greenup County.  Over 2000 customers lost electricity in Carter County and several hundred in Greenup County.||The storm ended mostly as rain during the predawn hours on the 5th.  Rain amounts peaked around 2 inches in portions of Carter and Greenup Counties to around 1.5 to 1.7 inches in Boyd County. Minor small stream flooding lead to minor river flooding along the Little Sandy River during the day on the 5th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The river at Grayson crested around 22.3 feet during the afternoon, a little more than a foot over flood stage.  No homes or businesses were flooded.  Roads were blocked and damaged by flood waters.

Accumulations of 3 to 5 inches were common across Greenup County, with one report of 7 inches in the South Shore vicinity.  Elsewhere, in northeast Kentucky, snow amounts were less.

Strong winds accompanied a cold front across northwest Kentucky, mainly along and north of a line from Marion to Calhoun. Immediately ahead of the cold front, strong southwest winds gusted as high as 55 mph during the early evening hours. Winds shifted into the west behind the front, with gusts reaching 45 to 50 mph at times through the evening. The highest measured wind gust outside of thunderstorms was 56 mph at the mesonet site near Marion in Crittenden County. The mesonet site near Calhoun recorded a peak gust of 49 mph ahead of the front. The peak gust at the Henderson City-County Airport was 49 mph, which occurred behind the front.

Strong winds downed trees and caused structural damage across portions of eastern Kentucky on December 24, 2014. A round of severe thunderstorms also caused wind damage during the early evening hours of Christmas Eve across portions of the area. Pike county suffered the most during the event, with several reports of downed trees coming from around the county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down on KY Hwy 1247 and another tree was blown down along Freedom Church Rd in Eubank.

Low pressure moving northeast from the Gulf states into the Mid-Atlantic pushed abundant moisture into the Ohio Valley on February 2nd, allowing a band of moderate to occasionally heavy snow to develop southeast of the Interstate 71 corridor during the overnight hours of February 2-3. The snow tapered off the following morning, with a tight gradient of snowfall amounts reported across parts of south-central Ohio and northeast Kentucky.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The observer south of Richwood measured 1.3 inches of snow. A spotter in Union measured an inch, while the CVG airport had a half inch of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast moving winter storm moved across the Ohio Valley on Tuesday evening, February 4th. Locations across northern Kentucky and southern Ohio started with heavy snow and transitioned to sleet and freezing rain. Significant ice accumulations caused tree damage and power outages to 5-10,000 people. Further north, snow mixed briefly with sleet, before changing to freezing rain as precipitation tapered off. The resulting 5 to 10 inches of snow and sleet accumulation in west-central and central Ohio. This storm brought widespread travel impacts with many schools and businesses being closed on Wednesday, February 5th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow, sleet, and freezing rain caused a large disruption to the region. Nearby measurements of 4 inches of snow were representative of northern sections of the county before the mixed precipitation cut snowfall totals significantly.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong winds downed trees and caused structural damage across portions of eastern Kentucky on December 24, 2014. A round of severe thunderstorms also caused wind damage during the early evening hours of Christmas Eve across portions of the area. Pike county suffered the most during the event, with several reports of downed trees coming from around the county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Shingles were blown off the roof of a house in Beattyville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In the wake of a reinforcing arctic cold front, high pressure was able to ridge east into northeast Kentucky during the predawn hours on the 24th.  Winds diminished to near calm and the sky remained mostly clear.  With a 2 to 4 inch snow cover, dawn temperatures of 5 to 10 below zero were common.  Specific preliminary data indicated the low temperature at Warnock and Louisa was 6 below zero.  Grayson observed 11 below zero.  Olive Hill was the coldest with a temperature of 12 degrees below zero.

Arctic air poured into northeast Kentucky on the 27th.  A clear night with diminishing winds resulted in temperatures on either side of the zero mark at dawn on the 28th.||The coldest dawn temperatures occurred on the 29th.  Preliminary data indicated the coldest temperature from the official cooperative observers network was 9 below zero at Olive Hill.| |Some of the other minimum temperature readings around dawn on the 29th from cooperative observers included 7 below zero at Warnock and 6 below zero west of Louisa.  Ashland observed a minimum temperature of 5 below zero. | |The cold wave lasted one more night, with readings of zero to 5 below zero around dawn on the 30th.  For example, Olive Hill observed minus 4. ||The 3 day cold wave finally broke on Thursday the 30th, as temperatures rose into the 35 to 40 degree range under sunshine. ||As the cold air was pouring in, a 12 inch water line broke along Carter Ave in Ashland Kentucky.  It caused about 5,000 customers in the Ashland, Catlettsburg, and Westwood vicinity to loose water.  The interruption of water service continued for several days.  The city of Ashland handed out bottled water.  The leak also drained the city's storage tank.

Wind gusts estimated in the 30 to 40 mph range occurred in a well mixed atmosphere during the afternoon of the 14th.  ||A Taco Bell restaurant sign fell down in Grayson. The sign landed onto the bed of a parked pick up truck.  Nobody was injured.  Apparently, the supporting pole broke where 2 sections came together, not at the ground level.

A strengthening low pressure system moved north into eastern Iowa and Wisconsin during the evening hours on the 20th.  The attending cold front raced east, up the Ohio River Valley.  Low level winds at 3 to 5 thousand feet above the ground were blowing at 50 to 70 mph.  Ahead of the front, temperatures were still in the 60s.  Surface dew points reached into the mid and upper 50s.||A narrow, but well defined, squall line formed on the leading edge of the prefrontal convection. It was oriented north to south while moving through northeast Kentucky just after 0000E on the 21st.  Strong wind gusts were associated with the squall line, mostly in the 40 to 50 mph range.  At least, 1 location had a stronger gust.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large branches and trees were blown down along Loss Lick Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon and evening hours of January 28, a small area of intense snow showers moved across portions of far eastern Kentucky. In a very short period of time, these snow showers dumped 1 to 2 inches of snow on portions of Harlan and Letcher counties. The snow quickly covered roads going over Pine Mountain, which caused numerous motorists to become stranded.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The state police post in Harlan reported that about 2 inches of snow had fallen on Pine Mountain. Several cars became stranded on the mountain and Highway 421 had to be temporarily shut down due to hazardous road conditions. It was still snowing heavily at the time this report was called in.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported that an inch of snow had fallen in Thornton since 430 this evening. It was still snowing hard at the time this report was called in.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A clipper system rapidly crossed from the northern plains into the Ohio Valley on the night of the 18th. Snow spread from west to east across the area and tapered off to flurries by the morning of the 19th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Cincinnati airport measures 1.2 inches of snow. The CoCoRaHS observer near Richwood measured an inch of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong temperature gradient existed from southeast to northeast across the mid Ohio Valley on the 4th.  An old snow cover over Ohio and extreme northern Kentucky helped to maintain surface temperatures slightly below freezing.||A low pressure storm system moved out of the Tennessee Valley late on the 4th.  The storm lifted northeast and up the Ohio Valley of eastern Ohio by dawn on the 5th.||A wintry mix of rain, sleet, and freezing rain developed across northeast Kentucky by 1900E on the 4th.  Ice from freezing rain accumulated from a quarter to a half inch across Carter and Greenup Counties before changing to plain rain.  In Lawrence County about a tenth of an inch of ice accumulated across the northern portion of the county mainly north of Route 32.  In the Knotty Poplar Fork area along the Carter County line, some trees were down with the ice reaching near a quarter of an inch.  The brunt of the storm was rain for Boyd and southern and eastern portions of Lawrence Counties.||Tree branches fell, including around Olive Hill of western Carter County and along the Tygarts Creek Valley in western Greenup County.  Over 2000 customers lost electricity in Carter County and several hundred in Greenup County.||The storm ended mostly as rain during the predawn hours on the 5th.  Rain amounts peaked around 2 inches in portions of Carter and Greenup Counties to around 1.5 to 1.7 inches in Boyd County. Minor small stream flooding lead to minor river flooding along the Little Sandy River during the day on the 5th.

The Ohio River continued to recede after heavy rainfall in late December. The heavy rainfall was caused by a slow-moving cold front that passed across the Lower Ohio and Mid Mississippi Valleys between December 20th and 22nd. Several low pressure systems moved north-northeast along the front, touching off numerous showers and thunderstorms. The river fell below flood stage in early January.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding lingered on the Ohio River through the first few days of 2014. Some low-lying woods and fields along the river were inundated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As colder air was filtering south, a strong mid level disturbance moved up the Ohio River.  Only a weak low pressure system was reflected at the surface, which passed to the south. Snow developed in southwestern Ohio by 0000E on the 21st.  The snow moved east, into northeast Kentucky during the predawn.||Northeast Kentucky was on the fringe of the main band of snow. Most accumulations were around 3 to 4 inches by late afternoon.  In the wake of the storm, clearing and light winds allowed temperatures to drop below zero for dawn on the 22nd.  Olive Hill was 11 degrees below zero for the coldest in northeast Kentucky, while Grayson was 7 below zero, and Ashland reached 3 below zero.

A killing freeze affected all of western Kentucky, effectively ending the growing season. Low temperatures were in the lower to mid 20's. Specific observed low temperatures included 22 degrees at Providence in Webster County, 23 degrees at the Paducah NWS office, 24 degrees at Madisonville, 25 degrees at Hopkinsville, Owensboro, and Marion, and 27 degrees at Mayfield and Murray. A surface high pressure ridge moved east across the Mid-Mississippi and Lower Ohio Valleys during the early morning hours. This high provided light winds and clear skies, setting the stage for a hard freeze.

A cyclone deepened rapidly during the early morning hours as it crossed the lower peninsula of Michigan. By 8 am on the 24th of November, surface pressure fell under 980mb near Traverse City. Across the Lower Ohio Valley, a cold front moved east of Interstate 65 around 8am. Steepening lapse rates during the morning hours both along and behind this boundary led to winds that occasionally gusted over 50 mph across southern Indiana and Kentucky.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Gradient winds blew a tree onto a house 5 miles southwest of Frankfort. The Frankfort ASOS measured a peak wind gust of 50 mph during the morning.

Widespread dense fog blanketed western Kentucky during the early morning hours. Visibility was reduced to one-quarter mile or less. The dense fog formed along a stationary front that extended from the southern Plains northeast across the Lower Ohio Valley. Areas of rain contributed to the saturation of the air near ground level.

Strong winds occurred along a cold front as it swept east across western Kentucky. The cold front trailed southward from a low pressure center near Chicago. Wind gusts reached 45 mph generally along and south of a line from Paducah to Madisonville. Winds gusted to 54 mph at the mesonet site near Princeton, 49 mph at Paducah, 47 mph at the mesonet site near Cadiz, 46 mph at the mesonet site about 6 miles southwest of Mayfield, 46 mph near Benton, and 45 mph near Madisonville and six miles north of Hopkinsville.

A warm front passed over the Ohio Valley. Due to a very cold ground, the rain showers in the wake of this warm front created a slushy layer of ice on road surfaces. While temperature sensors climbed above freezing in most locations, the rain still froze on area roadways. Numerous accidents and road closures were the result of this wintery weather.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Icy road conditions were found in spots across the county. One person was killed while travelling northbound on I-71. Their vehicle hit an icy spot on a bridge and flipped in the median near mile marker 55. They were not wearing a seat belt and were ejected from the vehicle.

Winds gusted to 54 mph at the mesonet site near Princeton, 49 mph at Paducah, 47 mph at the mesonet site near Cadiz, 46 mph at the mesonet site about 6 miles southwest of Mayfield, 46 mph near Benton, and 45 mph near Madisonville and six miles north of Hopkinsville.

A warm front passed over the Ohio Valley. Due to a very cold ground, the rain showers in the wake of this warm front created a slushy layer of ice on road surfaces. While temperature sensors climbed above freezing in most locations, the rain still froze on area roadways. Numerous accidents and road closures were the result of this wintery weather.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Icy road conditions were found in spots across the county, and the AA highway was closed for a period of time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: For a few hours during the early morning hours of January 2, 2014, light freezing rain created a coating of ice on numerous roadways around eastern Kentucky. Even though the accumulations of ice were miniscule, a few hundredths of an inch or less in most cases, the impacts were significant. The icy roads caused numerous car accidents in many counties around the area. Unfortunately, a number of people were injured in these accidents, a few fatally. This winter weather event provides a great example of how even the smallest accumulations of ice on roadways can create enormous impacts, particularly during the morning or evening rush.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A car accident that resulted in an injury occurred around 7 am on Highway 1304 in Girdler. The accident was by roads that were made slick due to freezing rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread dense fog blanketed western Kentucky during the early morning hours. Visibility was reduced to one-quarter mile or less. The dense fog formed along a stationary front that extended from the southern Plains northeast across the Lower Ohio Valley. Areas of rain contributed to the saturation of the air near ground level.

A strong upper level disturbance brought moisture and a shot of colder air to the region for Halloween into November 1st behind a strong cold front. The system initially brought rain to the region, but temperatures dropped overnight and rain began to mix with and change to snow in some areas. Locations of 1500 feet elevation and above received an inch or more of snow as did some locations near the Daniel Boone National Forest. The highest elevations along the Virginia border saw upwards of a half foot of snow, with the heavy wet snow downing trees on Pine and Black Mountains. Many of the deeper valley locations only experienced a brief mix of rain and snow and did not receive any accumulation.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts ranged from a dusting in most locations up to 2.2 inches in Clover Bottom.

An arctic cold front barreled across eastern Kentucky late Sunday January 5th ushering in what would be the coldest temperatures and wind chills experienced in 20 years. Gusty winds up to 50 mph along the front created a few isolated power outages. Rain falling in advance of the arctic front quickly changed over to snow by midnight Sunday night. By dawn on Monday, many areas had received from 1 to 3 inches of snow, with up to 5 inches on Black Mountain. ||The big story was the cold.  Temperatures by Tuesday morning January 7th ranged from 2 below zero to 9 below zero in the valleys of eastern Kentucky, down to 17 below zero at the top of Black Mountain. Gusty west to northwest winds combined with these temperatures to produce wind chills in the 15 to 30 below range for most locations, with the highest terrain along the Virginia border dropping even lower. The last time there were widespread below zero temperatures in eastern Kentucky was in the middle 1990s.||Several southeast Kentucky counties experienced water outages from the cold that lasted up to a week.  The hardest hit counties were Perry and Floyd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Kentucky Mesonet Station located five miles north of Hindman reported a wind chill of 27 below zero.

A killing freeze affected all of western Kentucky, effectively ending the growing season. Low temperatures were in the lower to mid 20's. Specific observed low temperatures included 22 degrees at Providence in Webster County, 23 degrees at the Paducah NWS office, 24 degrees at Madisonville, 25 degrees at Hopkinsville, Owensboro, and Marion, and 27 degrees at Mayfield and Murray. A surface high pressure ridge moved east across the Mid-Mississippi and Lower Ohio Valleys during the early morning hours. This high provided light winds and clear skies, setting the stage for a hard freeze.

A fast moving clipper system moved up the Ohio River Valley during the evening of the 14th.  A mixture of rain and snow changed over to all snow by 2300E in northeast Kentucky.  In the wake of the weak low pressure system, colder air rushed in during the predawn hours on the 15th.  The snow diminished by dawn.||Acccumulations of 3 to 4 inches occurred in Greenup County with only 1 to 3 inches further south and east, in Carter, Boyd, and Lawrence.

As colder air was filtering south, a strong mid level disturbance moved up the Ohio River.  Only a weak low pressure system was reflected at the surface, which passed to the south. Snow developed in southwestern Ohio by 0000E on the 21st.  The snow moved east, into northeast Kentucky during the predawn.||Northeast Kentucky was on the fringe of the main band of snow. Most accumulations were around 3 to 4 inches by late afternoon.  In the wake of the storm, clearing and light winds allowed temperatures to drop below zero for dawn on the 22nd.  Olive Hill was 11 degrees below zero for the coldest in northeast Kentucky, while Grayson was 7 below zero, and Ashland reached 3 below zero.

An unseasonably early arctic airmass invaded the Lower Ohio Valley November 12th, setting the stage for an early winter storm across southern Indiana and northern Kentucky northeast of Louisville. Rain changed to snow during the evening of the 16th of November as colder air filtered south as weak low pressure traversed Tennessee. Occasional snow, moderate at times, fell across northern Kentucky from Trimble County through Covington and the Cincinnati area through around dawn. In general, a thin swath of 3 to locally over 5 inches of snow fell across Trimble County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Four inches of snow was measured near Bedford.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: For a few hours during the early morning hours of January 2, 2014, light freezing rain created a coating of ice on numerous roadways around eastern Kentucky. Even though the accumulations of ice were miniscule, a few hundredths of an inch or less in most cases, the impacts were significant. The icy roads caused numerous car accidents in many counties around the area. Unfortunately, a number of people were injured in these accidents, a few fatally. This winter weather event provides a great example of how even the smallest accumulations of ice on roadways can create enormous impacts, particularly during the morning or evening rush.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were a total of 13 vehicle accidents reported in Rockcastle county, 3 of which involved injuries. The accidents occurred as the result of icy road conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A killing freeze affected all of western Kentucky, effectively ending the growing season. Low temperatures were in the lower to mid 20's. Specific observed low temperatures included 22 degrees at Providence in Webster County, 23 degrees at the Paducah NWS office, 24 degrees at Madisonville, 25 degrees at Hopkinsville, Owensboro, and Marion, and 27 degrees at Mayfield and Murray. A surface high pressure ridge moved east across the Mid-Mississippi and Lower Ohio Valleys during the early morning hours. This high provided light winds and clear skies, setting the stage for a hard freeze.

A strong upper-level disturbance moved through the Ohio Valley Friday evening, February 14th, ending on Saturday morning, February 15th. Surface low pressure crossed east across the state of Kentucky at the same time, allowing for an extended period of snow to develop.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The observer 2 miles south of Richwood and the Cincinnati airport both measured 3.8 inches of snow.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Four and a half inches of snow fell on the south side of Brooksville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe weather outbreak occurred across eastern Kentucky during the late afternoon and evening hours of October 7th. Three confirmed tornadoes occurred in Bath and Pike counties (2 in Bath and 1 in Pike). These twisters caused varying degrees of damage to trees, power lines, and various structures in both counties. These tornadoes were small and extremely short lived, which made them quite difficult to detect on radar. Numerous reports of large hail and wind damage were also received during this event. Some minor flooding occurred where numerous thunderstorms tracked across the same areas. The storms formed in a warm and only slightly unstable air mass that had set up across central and eastern Kentucky. The primary trigger for the severe and tornadic storms was a very strong and dynamic middle and upper level wind field that was present over the region. The rapid change in wind speed with height caused most every thunderstorm that formed on this day to rotate. A few of these circulations were able to reach the surface in Bath and Pike counties where the tornadoes were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down along KY 191.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast moving winter storm moved across the Ohio Valley on Tuesday evening, February 4th. Locations across northern Kentucky and southern Ohio started with heavy snow and transitioned to sleet and freezing rain. Significant ice accumulations caused tree damage and power outages to 5-10,000 people. Further north, snow mixed briefly with sleet, before changing to freezing rain as precipitation tapered off. The resulting 5 to 10 inches of snow and sleet accumulation in west-central and central Ohio. This storm brought widespread travel impacts with many schools and businesses being closed on Wednesday, February 5th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow, sleet, and freezing rain caused a large disruption to the region. Two to three inches of snow were found across the county before the mixed precipitation cut snowfall totals significantly.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front passed over the Ohio Valley. Due to a very cold ground, the rain showers in the wake of this warm front created a slushy layer of ice on road surfaces. While temperature sensors climbed above freezing in most locations, the rain still froze on area roadways. Numerous accidents and road closures were the result of this wintery weather.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Icy road conditions were found in spots across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surge of cold air worked into the Ohio Valley with an upper level disturbance pivoting through the region on Sunday night, November 16th. This cold surge changed any rain that was in the area to snow overnight for areas west of the I-75 corridor. East of this line, the changeover to snow did not occur until Monday and there were significantly lower snowfall amounts recorded here.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A report from Piner showed that 5 inches of snow fell there. A spotter south of Independence measured 4.5 inches, and another public report from Erlanger measured 3.8 inches.

Some minor flooding occurred where numerous thunderstorms tracked across the same areas. The storms formed in a warm and only slightly unstable air mass that had set up across central and eastern Kentucky. The primary trigger for the severe and tornadic storms was a very strong and dynamic middle and upper level wind field that was present over the region. The rapid change in wind speed with height caused most every thunderstorm that formed on this day to rotate. A few of these circulations were able to reach the surface in Bath and Pike counties where the tornadoes were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down on Hwy 769 between mile markers 7 and 8 and along Hwy 192 between mile markers 13 and 14.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surge of cold air worked into the Ohio Valley with an upper level disturbance pivoting through the region on Sunday night, November 16th. This cold surge changed any rain that was in the area to snow overnight for areas west of the I-75 corridor. East of this line, the changeover to snow did not occur until Monday and there were significantly lower snowfall amounts recorded here.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Based on nearby surrounding observations, much of northern Owen County is estimated to have received 4 inches of snow.

Snow showers overspread the Ohio Valley on Friday morning, January 17th as a result from an arctic cold front crossing the region. A weak surface low developed along the front and enhanced snow showers in spots.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter south of Union measured 2 inches of snow. The Cincinnati airport measured 1.4 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moving through the Tennessee Valley combined with a cold front dropping down across the Ohio Valley to produce widespread freezing rain, sleet and snow across the area. The precipitation remained mainly snow along and north of Interstate 70. However, to the south, the precipitation began as rain and freezing rain before changing to sleet and then snow through the afternoon and evening hours of March 2nd. Snow then continued along and south of the Ohio River through much of the night and on into the morning hours of March 3rd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Four and a half inches of snow fell 2 miles south of Richmond. Two tenths of an inch of ice was measured in Burlington and in Union. Interstate 275 was closed for 2 hours and numerous wrecks were reported across the region.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong winds downed trees and caused structural damage across portions of eastern Kentucky on December 24, 2014. A round of severe thunderstorms also caused wind damage during the early evening hours of Christmas Eve across portions of the area. Pike county suffered the most during the event, with several reports of downed trees coming from around the county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down on Hwy 979 in Harold.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong warm air advection in the Ohio Valley helped snow to develop in northeast Kentucky just before dawn on the 25th. Lulls in the snowfall developed during the midday.  A strong cold front and its associated mid level disturbance helped trigger more showery snow during the late afternoon into the early evening.  Snow accumulations of 2 to 3 inches were common across northeast Kentucky.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow began during the early morning hours and became moderate at times before ending in the afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Francisville, there was 4.3 inches of snow measured. Nearby estimates from Indiana and Ohio suggested over 4 inches of snow affected northern sections of the county, including the airport, where 4.2 inches fell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: For a few hours during the early morning hours of January 2, 2014, light freezing rain created a coating of ice on numerous roadways around eastern Kentucky. Even though the accumulations of ice were miniscule, a few hundredths of an inch or less in most cases, the impacts were significant. The icy roads caused numerous car accidents in many counties around the area. Unfortunately, a number of people were injured in these accidents, a few fatally. This winter weather event provides a great example of how even the smallest accumulations of ice on roadways can create enormous impacts, particularly during the morning or evening rush.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Both lanes of Highway 92 East at the McCreary/Whitley county line were closed due to icy road conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous thunderstorms developed along and ahead of a slow moving cold front during the afternoon and evening. These storms were capable of producing damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down and was blocking Route 8 due to thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed in an unstable air mass ahead of a cold front. These thunderstorms were capable of producing large hail and damaging winds.

A major winter storm pummeled portions of eastern Kentucky on February 12th and 13th. The highest snow fall totals were measured in the higher terrain of our Virginia border counties, where 8 to 14 inches of snow fell. All or portions of the tier of counties just to the west of the border counties received 5 to 11 inches of snow. The snow first began to accumulate during the early evening hours of Wednesday, February 12th and didn't stop causing issues until late on the morning of Thursday, February 13th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts of up to four inches were observed near Paintsville and over the southeast part of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Minor river flooding occurred in response to a combination of snowmelt and heavy rain. A warm front lifted north, helping temperatures reach near 70 degrees by the 20th. Ice and snowmelt ran off into the river. This runoff, combined with heavy showers and thunderstorms ahead of a cold front, caused a rise in the river.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding occurred along the Ohio River. Low-lying woods and fields near the river were underwater. The flooding continued into early March.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms intensified within a zone of strong southerly low level winds that provided abundant warmth and moisture. The storms formed near a warm front that was draped across southeast Missouri, southern Illinois, and western Kentucky. The storms occurred within a moist and moderately unstable air mass along and behind the warm front. The strong moisture feed contributed to torrential downpours that produced isolated flash flooding in the Henderson and Owensboro areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A side street was flooded in Stanley. Cars were turning around.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rain overspread east Kentucky Friday evening as a strengthening area of low pressure tracked across the state. The rain changed to snow from northwest to southeast during the night. The heaviest snowfall amounts fell north of I-64 with up to 4 inches of snow reported in Fleming County. Generally an inch or less of snow was reported south of I-64, but quickly falling temperatures freezing moisture on the roads resulted in slick and treacherous travel conditions even with the light snowfall amounts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three to four inches of snow fell across northern Fleming County, with lesser amounts to the south.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees were downed near Perry Park due to thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed along and south of a warm front during the afternoon. Some of these storms had the potential to become severe. The main threat from these storms was damaging winds.

Thunderstorms intensified within a zone of strong southerly low level winds that provided abundant warmth and moisture. The storms formed near a warm front that was draped across southeast Missouri, southern Illinois, and western Kentucky. The storms occurred within a moist and moderately unstable air mass along and behind the warm front. The strong moisture feed contributed to torrential downpours that produced isolated flash flooding in the Henderson and Owensboro areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain over the previous 24 hours caused flooding of some roads, a few of which were impassable.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong northerly winds moved in behind a cold front late at night and continued through the morning hours. Along and south of a line from Marion to Madisonville to Central City, winds gusted to around 45 mph. The highest measured wind gust in western Kentucky was 47 mph at the Marion-Crittenden County airport. The second highest measured gust was 45 mph at both the Paducah airport and at a mesonet site near Central City (in Muhlenberg County).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of strong to severe thunderstorms, associated with an eastward moving cold front, caused significant wind damage across Bell, Harlan, Johnson, and Whitley counties during the afternoon hours of April 28th. The worst of the damage occurred in Harlan county where a storm damage survey revealed that estimated straight line winds of 90 mph downed trees and power lines across the county. Three people were injured in Evarts as a result of the severe winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A county employee was struck by lightning. The person was taken to a hospital but there were no details available on specific injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure moved east across the Ohio Valley, bringing unseasonably cold air and widespread freezing temperatures. Lows were from 28 to 32 degrees at many locations in western Kentucky. The coldest observed temperature was 28 degrees at the Henderson airport. Other lows included 30 degrees at Marion (Crittenden County), and 32 degrees at Paducah, Mayfield, and Providence.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure moved east across the Ohio Valley, bringing unseasonably cold air and widespread freezing temperatures. Lows were from 28 to 32 degrees at many locations in western Kentucky. The coldest observed temperature was 28 degrees at the Henderson airport. Other lows included 30 degrees at Marion (Crittenden County), and 32 degrees at Paducah, Mayfield, and Providence.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Wind chill values dropped to between minus 10 and minus 15 across parts of northwest Kentucky, mainly the Henderson and Owensboro areas. The lowest wind chills occurred during the early morning hours. At Owensboro, the actual low temperature was 4 degrees above zero. A strong Arctic high pressure system over the Plains states funneled in bitterly cold air on gusty northwest winds.

Strong northerly winds moved in behind a cold front late at night and continued through the morning hours. Along and south of a line from Marion to Madisonville to Central City, winds gusted to around 45 mph. The highest measured wind gust in western Kentucky was 47 mph at the Marion-Crittenden County airport. The second highest measured gust was 45 mph at both the Paducah airport and at a mesonet site near Central City (in Muhlenberg County).

For a few hours during the early morning hours of January 2, 2014, light freezing rain created a coating of ice on numerous roadways around eastern Kentucky. Even though the accumulations of ice were miniscule, a few hundredths of an inch or less in most cases, the impacts were significant. The icy roads caused numerous car accidents in many counties around the area. Unfortunately, a number of people were injured in these accidents, a few fatally. This winter weather event provides a great example of how even the smallest accumulations of ice on roadways can create enormous impacts, particularly during the morning or evening rush.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Highway 80 between Hazard and Hindman was very slick due to freezing rain with several car accidents reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms intensified within a zone of strong southerly low level winds that provided abundant warmth and moisture. The storms formed near a warm front that was draped across southeast Missouri, southern Illinois, and western Kentucky. The storms occurred within a moist and moderately unstable air mass along and behind the warm front. The strong moisture feed contributed to torrential downpours that produced isolated flash flooding in the Henderson and Owensboro areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water was over several intersections along U.S. Highway 41A in Henderson.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow showers overspread the Ohio Valley on Friday morning, January 17th as a result from an arctic cold front crossing the region. A weak surface low developed along the front and enhanced snow showers in spots.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter south of Falmouth measured 2 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions that had started in central Kentucky earlier in the summer slowly spread west, encompassing parts of western Kentucky by late July. The area of moderate drought conditions extended from Murray to Hopkinsville and Elkton. July monthly rainfall totals were only 25 to 50 percent of normal in the drought area. Impacts of the drought were primarily agricultural. A report from Todd County indicated corn and early-planted soybean yields were projected to be reduced by about 50 percent. The same report from Todd County stated pastures were brown. During the latter half of July, high pressure in the upper levels of the atmosphere controlled much of the central part of the United States. This high produced dry and cool northwest winds over Kentucky for much of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread strong to severe thunderstorms downed trees and power lines and brought locally heavy rainfall to eastern Kentucky on June 10th, 2014. A number of homes, barns, and various outbuildings also suffered varying degrees of damage due to severe thunderstorm wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees were blown down along Evelyn Rd and Hwy 2453.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: By dawn on the 4th, a cold front sagged south to near the Interstate 64 corridor in northeast Kentucky.  A strong low pressure for early June moved east through the Midwest, along the frontal boundary, during the day on the 4th.  Dew points were in the upper 60s and lower 70s near and south of the front.  The disturbance passed to the east by early on the 5th. ||Several rounds of showers and thunderstorms passed northeast Kentucky during the afternoon and evening hours on the 4th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of disturbances that passed across the Lower Ohio River valley and central and eastern Kentucky helped spawn strong to severe thunderstorms across eastern Kentucky on August 21st. Warm moist air from the Gulf of Mexico, coupled with cool dry air aloft, set the stage for storm formation as an upper level system moved past the area during the afternoon and early evening hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: More flooding occurred across eastern Kentucky during the afternoon and evening hours of September 6th. Thunderstorms formed across central Kentucky ahead of a cold front during the afternoon hours on September 6th. As these storms moved eastward, they ingested moisture rich air that was already in place across eastern Kentucky. This, along with the relatively slow movement of the storms, allowed the storms to produce very heavy rainfall. This rainfall lead to flash flooding across a number of counties across eastern Kentucky on this day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Wolfe county search and rescue responded to 50 rock climbers stranded by flood waters in Muir valley. The middle fork of Lower Devil Creek swelled to over 8 feet deep and 50 feet wide in spots, inundating bridges and stranding the climbers.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On June 10th, a deep, nearly cutoff 500mb low was located over Missouri.  Ample moisture with PWATs over 1.5 inches moved northward from the Tennessee Valley into central Kentucky.  Cloud cover during much of the day limited instability along and west of Interstate 65.  However, partly cloudy skies allowed temperatures to rise into the mid 80s across the Bluegrass and Lake Cumberland Regions.  Moderate southwesterly mid-level flow combined with steep low level lapse rates allowed a line of thunderstorms to develop north-to-south along Interstate 65 during the early afternoon hours.  This line produced a couple of bowing segments that brought isolated damaging winds to much of southeastern and eastern Kentucky.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Richmond law enforcement reported trees down near the intersections of Mt. Vernon Road and Liberty Avenue, Highway 421 and Battlefield Road, and near the Waco Baptist Church.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moderate instability and a weak disturbance combined to produce scattered thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening hours on June 23rd. Due to steep low level lapse rates and sufficient temperature dewpoint depressions, some storms produced damaging winds across central Kentucky. One storm in Grayson County briefly acquired supercell characteristics and produced ping pong sized hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter reported high winds and ping pong sized hail. This storm was the only one during the whole afternoon that briefly acquired supercell characteristics.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding occurred along Arkansas Creek, Click Branch, Mare Creek, Prater Creek and Corn Fork. Prater Creek flooded nearby buildings and residences.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A prolonged period of late summer heat and humidity followed an unusually cool July. For eight consecutive days, the heat index peaked between 100 and 110 degrees each afternoon. The heat index reached as high as 115 at some locations in the Henderson/Owensboro area on the 23rd. Surface high pressure over the central Gulf coast region from the 20th to the 24th moved to the southern Appalachian Mountains by the 25th. This high produced a continuous supply of heat and humidity. The triple-digit heat indices ended a couple days sooner along the Tennessee border.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rounds of showers and thunderstorms formed in the weak northwest flow aloft during the late afternoon hours of the 22nd.  Rain amounts of 2 to 4 inches in 1 to 2 hours were estimated over parts of Boyd County during the early evening.  The heaviest rain appeared to be around Cannonsburg.  In Lawrence County the estimated rain maximum was around 2 to 2.5 inches.  These downpours caused flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Small streams along Route 210, such as Lick Creek, flooded roads.   In the Nats Creek vicinity near the Martin County line, roads were flooded.  No homes were flooded.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Both urban and rural flash flooding occurred during the early evening hours of a Friday night.  Numerous streets in Ashland were flooded with several vehicles flooded.  Water even pooled on their high school football field from the overwhelmed drainage system, causing the first game of the season to be canceled.   ||Vehicles were also flooded on roads around Summit and Ironville. In Ironville, some people were trapped in houses surrounded by water.  In the Cannonsburg vicinity, streams along Route 3 such as Laurel Creek and Lower Chadwick Creek flooded.   One house had its first floor flooded, and its residents were rescued from the second floor.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong jet began to carve out an unseasonably deep trough across the upper midwest early on the 27th of July. Low pressure deepened as it moved across the eastern Great Lakes late on that same day.  Ahead of this surface feature, very warm temperatures and high humidities developed across the Lower Ohio Valley during the afternoon of the 26th. Strong northwesterly flow aloft and a southwesterly low level jet aided in the development of several rounds of severe storms that brought scattered reports of large hail and damaging winds for an almost continuous 24 hour period, stretching from the evening hours on the 26th through late evening on the 27th.  A bow echo moved southeast of the Ohio River during the evening of the 26th, bringing widespread damaging winds and isolated large hail. During the early morning hours of the 27th, additional clusters of strong storms moved across central Kentucky. By late morning, a supercell developed northwest of Lexington, bringing very large hail and a damaging microburst in Lexington. Finally, additional supercells developed during the late afternoon across the Lake Cumberland Region and eastern Kentucky.

Early on Sunday, August 10th, a nearly stationary front stretched from weak low pressure in Arkansas northeast along the Ohio River. This boundary became a focus for repeated episodes of slow moving heavy thunderstorms throughout the day, and even into Monday. During the morning hours on Sunday, a cluster of storms developed near Lexington and brought flooding to several counties in the Bluegrass Region. For the date, the ASOS site at the Lexington Airport measured a record 5.38 inches of rain. Additional scattered storms across central Kentucky Sunday afternoon brought more flooding. Finally, some afternoon storms on Monday the 11th brought additional heavy rainfall to portions of the eastern Bluegrass Region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Richmond law enforcement reported water across the Richmond Bypass, associated with Interstate 75. The road was still passable.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved southeast toward the Ohio Valley during the afternoon. Clusters of thunderstorms formed within a weak pre-frontal trough extending from northeast Kentucky southwestward across west central Kentucky. A cluster of storms over the Pennyrile region of western Kentucky strengthened as it interacted with a more moist and unstable boundary layer. The storms were embedded within a band of strong westerlies aloft, with deep-layer bulk wind shear values of 40 to 50 knots. Some of the storms produced large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large hailstones slightly larger than golf balls fell in Calhoun. The hailstones were photographed by at least two weather enthusiasts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread severe thunderstorms affected portions of eastern Kentucky on July 27th in advance of a strong cold front ushering in a fall-like air mass. Isolated storms moved across the area during the late morning hours as they moved out of Indiana and Ohio. The more significant severe weather episode occurred during the afternoon and evening hours south of the Mountain Parkway. The second round of storms began to form as warm moist air off the Gulf of Mexico moved northward and interacted with a frontal boundary that extended from south central Indiana into central Kentucky. Numerous reports of large hail between quarter and softball size were received by the weather office. The storms also blew down large numbers of trees across the area. The hardest areas were Clay, Leslie, McCreary, and Whitley counties, where numerous reports of downed trees, giant hail and even one tornado were received. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moderate instability and a weak disturbance combined to produce scattered thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening hours on June 23rd. Due to steep low level lapse rates and sufficient temperature dewpoint depressions, some storms produced damaging winds across central Kentucky. One storm in Grayson County briefly acquired supercell characteristics and produced ping pong sized hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds damaged the roofs of both the water treatment plant and the Southern States Building.  Several trees were downed across power lines in the Cynthiana area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave moved across the western Great Lakes within a northwesterly 500mb flow regime that also affected the Lower Ohio Valley June 5. During the afternoon hours, a broken line of storms developed across southern Illinois and moved southeast across western and west central Kentucky. This line had several breaks with brief supercell structures that exhibited rotation. Several of these storms produced weak tornadoes across western Kentucky, as well as some scattered wind damage and funnel cloud reports near the Bowling Green area. A second small area of thunderstorms brought isolated severe wind gusts and minor flooding across the western Bluegrass Region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain shut down a road briefly near the BP Gas Station on Catnip Hill.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Early on Sunday, August 10th, a nearly stationary front stretched from weak low pressure in Arkansas northeast along the Ohio River. This boundary became a focus for repeated episodes of slow moving heavy thunderstorms throughout the day, and even into Monday. During the morning hours on Sunday, a cluster of storms developed near Lexington and brought flooding to several counties in the Bluegrass Region. For the date, the ASOS site at the Lexington Airport measured a record 5.38 inches of rain. Additional scattered storms across central Kentucky Sunday afternoon brought more flooding. Finally, some afternoon storms on Monday the 11th brought additional heavy rainfall to portions of the eastern Bluegrass Region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: West Hickman Creek exceeded bankfull and inundated its floodplain near Veterans Park.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved southeast toward the Ohio Valley during the afternoon. Clusters of thunderstorms formed within a weak pre-frontal trough extending from northeast Kentucky southwestward across west central Kentucky. A cluster of storms over the Pennyrile region of western Kentucky strengthened as it interacted with a more moist and unstable boundary layer. The storms were embedded within a band of strong westerlies aloft, with deep-layer bulk wind shear values of 40 to 50 knots. Some of the storms produced large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Pea to dime-size hail was reported at exit 44 of the Pennyrile Parkway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak low pressure system moved generally eastward across southern Missouri to the confluence of the Mississippi and Ohio Rivers. Ahead of this low, clusters of thunderstorms moved east-southeast across western Kentucky. These storms were aided by a small 500 mb shortwave trough sliding east-southeast across the Lower Ohio Valley. The storms occurred in a region of moderately strong wind shear. Winds were generally from 20 to 30 knots throughout the lower and mid levels of the atmosphere, but with strong directional shear. Rich low-level moisture was present, with surface dew points in the lower 70's. A couple of weak tornadoes occurred in this moist and moderately sheared environment.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This small tornado blew down several small trees and limbs in multiple directions along County Road 1011. There were numerous reports of this brief touchdown that snapped trees and damaged a cornfield. Peak winds were estimated near 75 mph.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An IFLOWS rain gauge measured 3.03 inches of rain in one hour ending at 3 pm.  As a result at least a foot of water was seen flowing across the junction of Highways 587 and 399 and both roads were closed.  An automated river level gauge on Sturgeon Creek recorded an eight foot rise in two and a half hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A flat shortwave moved southeast across Indiana during the afternoon hours on June 20th. Although this wave had no surface reflection and brought only light westerly winds both at the surface and aloft, sufficient instability and the presence of PWATs near 1.75 inches allowed numerous thunderstorms to develop by mid afternoon across southern Indiana and along the Ohio River. Most storms only brought brief heavy rain and wind gusts up to 50 mph. However, a stronger cell near Frankfort produced a damaging microburst.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Local law enforcement reported that a tree fell across a house in east Frankfort. Several people sent pictures via social media of trees down in the Silver Lake neighborhood just east of Frankfort.  A spotter with a weather station recorded a wind gust of 55 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On June 10th, a deep, nearly cutoff 500mb low was located over Missouri.  Ample moisture with PWATs over 1.5 inches moved northward from the Tennessee Valley into central Kentucky.  Cloud cover during much of the day limited instability along and west of Interstate 65.  However, partly cloudy skies allowed temperatures to rise into the mid 80s across the Bluegrass and Lake Cumberland Regions.  Moderate southwesterly mid-level flow combined with steep low level lapse rates allowed a line of thunderstorms to develop north-to-south along Interstate 65 during the early afternoon hours.  This line produced a couple of bowing segments that brought isolated damaging winds to much of southeastern and eastern Kentucky.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The fire department reported multiple trees downed along Highway 52 several miles east of Richmond.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A small cluster of severe thunderstorms that originated over southeast Missouri moved east-southeast across the southern tip of Illinois, then into the Paducah area of western Kentucky. An isolated damaging wind gust to near 65 mph was measured at the Paducah airport just before the storms weakened. The storms occurred during the heat of the afternoon along a weakening surface trough that extended southward from the Great Lakes region. The storms were aided by a mid-level shortwave trough moving east-southeast toward the Lower Ohio Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust to 63 mph was measured by the automated observing system at Barkley Regional Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered strong thunderstorms brought heavy rain and several instances of flooding to eastern Kentucky during the afternoon and evening hours of September 4th. Thunderstorms with heavy rainfall formed and moved across the area as a mid level wave of low pressure moved across the area and tapped into large amounts of low level moisture that had been in place across the Ohio and Tennessee Valleys.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Localized slow moving thunderstorm produced an estimated three to four inches of rainfall on the Floyd/Pike County line.  Runoff from these storms produced flash flooding on Cow Creek which nearly entered trailers at a trailer court off Cow Creek Road. Daniels Creek also flooded with water entering a parked vehicle and a creek near Harold flooded an adjacent property and driveway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread severe thunderstorms affected portions of eastern Kentucky on July 27th in advance of a strong cold front ushering in a fall-like air mass. Isolated storms moved across the area during the late morning hours as they moved out of Indiana and Ohio. The more significant severe weather episode occurred during the afternoon and evening hours south of the Mountain Parkway. The second round of storms began to form as warm moist air off the Gulf of Mexico moved northward and interacted with a frontal boundary that extended from south central Indiana into central Kentucky. Numerous reports of large hail between quarter and softball size were received by the weather office. The storms also blew down large numbers of trees across the area. The hardest areas were Clay, Leslie, McCreary, and Whitley counties, where numerous reports of downed trees, giant hail and even one tornado were received. The tornado occurred in Leslie county along with giant softball size hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down and across Blankey Branch Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unstable airmass resulted in scattered thunderstorm development through the late afternoon and early evening hours. A few of the storms produced damaging winds, locally heavy rainfall and isolated tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down on Craigs Creek Lake where substantial damage occurred to a large dock near the marina entrance along US Route 42. A section of roofing and the associated supporting structure measuring approximately 75 by 75 feet was destroyed and deposited immediately to the southwest of the original location. The entire dock measured approximately 100 by 225 feet and was attached to a number of metal poles anchored into the lake bottom. These poles were approximately 6 inches in diameter and were all bent at least several degrees toward the southwest, indicating the entire structure was moved in that direction. This movement was perpendicular to the apparent storm motion at that time as ascertained from radar.||Tree damage was also observed on an island approximately 150 yards east of this dock. Several 4 to 8 inch diameter trees had fallen toward the northwest. Additional trees of similar size were also noted to be downed along the western shore of the lake near Knox Lillard Road, approximately a half mile southeast of the intersection with US Route 42. Roof covering and several concrete cinder blocks securing a patio roof were lifted from a structure along the shoreline. This material was deposited 50 to 100 feet south of the original location.||The tornado ended just before Hillandale Drive where some minor tree damage occurred along the shoreline.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Early on Sunday, August 10th, a nearly stationary front stretched from weak low pressure in Arkansas northeast along the Ohio River. This boundary became a focus for repeated episodes of slow moving heavy thunderstorms throughout the day, and even into Monday. During the morning hours on Sunday, a cluster of storms developed near Lexington and brought flooding to several counties in the Bluegrass Region. For the date, the ASOS site at the Lexington Airport measured a record 5.38 inches of rain. Additional scattered storms across central Kentucky Sunday afternoon brought more flooding. Finally, some afternoon storms on Monday the 11th brought additional heavy rainfall to portions of the eastern Bluegrass Region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm dumped heavy rain on Bowling Green around dawn.  Several inches of water collected on a few roads across the campus of Western Kentucky University.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong jet began to carve out an unseasonably deep trough across the upper midwest early on the 27th of July. Low pressure deepened as it moved across the eastern Great Lakes late on that same day.  Ahead of this surface feature, very warm temperatures and high humidities developed across the Lower Ohio Valley during the afternoon of the 26th. Strong northwesterly flow aloft and a southwesterly low level jet aided in the development of several rounds of severe storms that brought scattered reports of large hail and damaging winds for an almost continuous 24 hour period, stretching from the evening hours on the 26th through late evening on the 27th.  A bow echo moved southeast of the Ohio River during the evening of the 26th, bringing widespread damaging winds and isolated large hail. During the early morning hours of the 27th, additional clusters of strong storms moved across central Kentucky. By late morning, a supercell developed northwest of Lexington, bringing very large hail and a damaging microburst in Lexington. Finally, additional supercells developed during the late afternoon across the Lake Cumberland Region and eastern Kentucky.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Significant flash flooding developed across portions of eastern Kentucky during the late afternoon and evening hours of August 22nd into the early morning hours of August 23rd. Clusters of slow moving thunderstorms moved repeatedly across the areas of Johnson, Floyd, Pike, and Martin counties. Other counties that experienced flooding were Montgomery, Powell, Wolfe, and Estill counties. The most widespread and devastating flooding, however, occurred across portions of Johnson, Floyd, and Pike counties. Floyd county was hit the hardest, particularly in a small section of north central Floyd where around 4 inches of rain fell in less than two hours. The flooding washed out roads in Estill, Floyd, and Johnson counties. At one point during the evening of August 22nd, numerous areas in and around Prestonsburg were completely under water with multiple cars and buildings surrounded by water at times. In general, 2 to 4 inches of rain fell in 90 minute to two hour period across Johnson, Floyd, and Pike counties, which lead to excessive runoff that quickly brought creeks and streams out of their banks and roadside culverts and storms drains to quickly overflow into adjacent roadways.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread flash flooding occurred across much of Johnson County during the evening of August 22nd as over three inches of rain fell in one to two hours.  Basements, garages and homes were flooded in numerous areas with multiple reports of road damage to private and public property.  Roads were completely blocked and in some cases were completely washed away.  There were also numerous reports of downed trees and electric lines due to mudslides.  Sixteen homes were classified as having major damage along with two businesses.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms that formed ahead of an approaching area of low pressure produced heavy rainfall that caused flash flooding across portions of eastern Kentucky on August 18th. Most of the flooding occurred in Breathitt, Knott, and Perry counties during the morning hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Over three inches of rainfall from slow moving thunderstorms produced flash flooding across the county.  Troublesome Creek was reported out of its banks from Hindman to Carrie, six inches of flowing water was reported over Highway 80, flooding at the junction of Highway 550 and 160 made the roads impassable, flood waters surrounded a house in Garner, the Hazard Community & Technical School campus was flooded in Hindman, and water was reported over the bumper of an SUV between Leburn and Hindman.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak low pressure system moved generally eastward across southern Missouri to the confluence of the Mississippi and Ohio Rivers. Ahead of this low, clusters of thunderstorms moved east-southeast across western Kentucky. These storms were aided by a small 500 mb shortwave trough sliding east-southeast across the Lower Ohio Valley. The storms occurred in a region of moderately strong wind shear. Winds were generally from 20 to 30 knots throughout the lower and mid levels of the atmosphere, but with strong directional shear. Rich low-level moisture was present, with surface dew points in the lower 70's. A couple of weak tornadoes occurred in this moist and moderately sheared environment.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This tornado was photographed by a sheriff's deputy and confirmed by a National Weather Service damage survey. Peak winds were estimated near 90 mph. There was minor roof and siding damage to three homes and a mobile home. Numerous large tree limbs were broken, and a couple of trees were uprooted. A large tree was down across a roadway. The path of the suction vortex was clearly evident in aerial photos of a cornfield. The tornado crossed Interstate 69, also known as the Pennyrile Parkway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure over the Great Lakes and a cold front extending southwest from it through the northwest Ohio Valley and into the middle of Missouri were the initial cause of severe thunderstorms that eventually moved across eastern Kentucky later in the evening of May 21st. Southeast of this weather system, plenty of fuel was building for convection in the form of dew points in the low to mid 60s and temperatures pushing 80 degrees. This helped to prime east Kentucky for showers and storms later in the evening and through the night. The main show later that evening was eventually also associated with ongoing storms in Ohio and Indiana both directly and as a cluster of new storms that formed on an old outflow boundary.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and power lines were blown down in Livingston.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread severe thunderstorms affected portions of eastern Kentucky on July 27th in advance of a strong cold front ushering in a fall-like air mass. Isolated storms moved across the area during the late morning hours as they moved out of Indiana and Ohio. The more significant severe weather episode occurred during the afternoon and evening hours south of the Mountain Parkway. The second round of storms began to form as warm moist air off the Gulf of Mexico moved northward and interacted with a frontal boundary that extended from south central Indiana into central Kentucky. Numerous reports of large hail between quarter and softball size were received by the weather office. The storms also blew down large numbers of trees across the area. The hardest areas were Clay, Leslie, McCreary, and Whitley counties, where numerous reports of downed trees, giant hail and even one tornado were received. The tornado occurred in Leslie county along with giant softball size hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down briefly in southern Leslie County visible from Highway 2009 around 424 PM on Sunday afternoon.  A consistent path up to a half mile wide of uprooted and snapped hardwood and softwood trees was observed descending down the Upper Double branch creek, crossing Highway 2009 and then ascending back up the adjacent hillside in a west-northwest to east-southeast direction. Uprooted trees damaged a cemetery but no habitable structures were damaged. Based on visible damage, maximum winds likely reached 110 mph in the tornado. The tornado touched down in a remote area and the only access was via Highway 2009, thus tornado specifics are limited to what could be observed from the road and information obtained from a local land owner.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak surface trough sagging south across southern Indiana sparked a thin broken line of thunderstorms during the late afternoon. These storms intensified and brought some strong wind gusts across several counties centered around Fort Knox, which is located in Hardin County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a home in Louisville, starting a fire in the attic.
